name: United Arab Emirates (الإمارات العربية المتحدة)
summary: The United Arab Emirates is a Gulf mixed-legal-system federation of seven emirates operating a dual personal-status framework: Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 establishes a Personal Status Law for non-Muslims (in force 1 February 2023, applicable to all non-Muslim residents and visitors regardless of nationality), while Federal Law 28/2005 (Personal Status Law) and its implementing regulations govern matters for Muslim residents under Maliki/Shafi'i jurisprudential framework with substantial reforms via Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 amendments. Custody (hadana) provisions differ between the two frameworks. The Federal Supreme Court (Al-Mahkama al-Ittihadiyya al-Ulya) is the apex federal court; Abu Dhabi and Dubai operate their own court systems with separate apex jurisdictions for emirate-level matters. The Dubai International Financial Centre (DIFC) and Abu Dhabi Global Market (ADGM) Courts operate common-law jurisdictions for commercial matters. Psychology profession is regulated under the Ministry of Health and Prevention (MOHAP) federal framework with separate Department of Health Abu Dhabi (DOH) and Dubai Health Authority (DHA) licensing requirements. UAE is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the welfare-of-the-child standard. UAE acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 on Child Abduction effective 1 August 2018.

statutory_framework.citation: Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 — Personal Status Law for Non-Muslims
statutory_framework.title: Personal Status Law for Non-Muslims
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute in force 1 February 2023 establishing a civil personal-status framework for non-Muslim residents and visitors regardless of nationality. Significant divergence from prior personal-law application of nationality law for non-Muslims. Includes joint custody provisions as the default.

statutory_framework.citation: Federal Law 28/2005 (Personal Status Law) as amended
statutory_framework.title: Personal Status Law 2005 — Muslim personal status
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute governing personal status for Muslims. Substantially amended by Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 amendments and Federal Decree-Law 8/2024 modernising custody, divorce, and inheritance provisions.

apex_courts.name: Federal Supreme Court (Al-Mahkama al-Ittihadiyya al-Ulya)
apex_courts.role: Apex federal court for civil, criminal, constitutional and administrative matters under federal jurisdiction. Dubai and Abu Dhabi operate separate emirate-level court systems with their own apex jurisdictions for emirate-level matters.

apex_courts.name: Abu Dhabi Court of Cassation + Dubai Court of Cassation
apex_courts.role: Emirate-level apex courts operating separately from the Federal Supreme Court for matters within Abu Dhabi and Dubai emirate jurisdictions.

professional_regulators.name: Ministry of Health and Prevention (MOHAP)
professional_regulators.role: Federal regulator for health professionals at federal level.

professional_regulators.name: Department of Health Abu Dhabi (DOH)
professional_regulators.role: Abu Dhabi-emirate health regulator operating separate licensing requirements for psychologists practising in Abu Dhabi.

professional_regulators.name: Dubai Health Authority (DHA)
professional_regulators.role: Dubai-emirate health regulator operating separate licensing requirements for psychologists practising in Dubai.

anonymisation_convention: UAE family-court decisions are anonymised per court practice; published decisions use initials or pseudonyms.

key_developments.title: UAE federation establishment + Sheikh Zayed founding-presidency + Constitution
key_developments.description: United Arab Emirates federation established 2 December 1971 from Trucial States — Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan founding president 1971-2004. Provisional Constitution 1971 (made permanent 1996) establishing federal framework. Substantively distinctive Gulf seven-emirate-federation framework affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Dubai International Financial Centre framework + common-law-enclave framework
key_developments.description: Dubai International Financial Centre (DIFC) framework 2004 with common-law DIFC Courts substantively distinctive Gulf common-law-enclave-within-civil-law-state framework. Subsequent ADGM 2013 substantively significant Abu Dhabi parallel-jurisdiction framework affecting subsequent cross-border-jurisdiction-practice.

key_developments.title: UAE ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child
key_developments.description: UAE ratified the UNCRC on 3 January 1997 (with reservations consistent with Islamic Sharia) — substantively significant child-rights framework integration. Foundational best-interests-of-the-child framework integration with subsequent 2005 Personal Status Law substantive doctrine.

key_developments.title: Federal Law 28/2005
key_developments.description: Federal Personal Status Law enacted 28 November 2005 governing personal status for Muslims drawing on Maliki/Shafi'i jurisprudential framework. Foundational substantive-family-law framework persisting through subsequent reform-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Wadeema's Law 2016 + child-protection framework consolidation
key_developments.description: Federal Law 3/2016 (Wadeema's Law / Children's Rights Law) named after deceased child — substantively significant Gulf comprehensive-children's-protection framework. Substantive child-protection-framework consolidation affecting subsequent family-law-implementation framework.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 accession
key_developments.description: UAE acceded to the Hague Convention 1980 on Child Abduction effective 1 August 2018 — substantively significant Hague Gulf accession. Distinguishes UAE from Saudi Arabia + Iran (non-Hague) within Gulf cluster.

key_developments.title: Abraham Accords + Israel-UAE-normalisation + geopolitical-realignment framework
key_developments.description: Abraham Accords signed 15 September 2020 substantively distinctive globally Israel-UAE-normalisation framework. Substantive geopolitical-realignment affecting subsequent cross-border-jurisdiction-practice. Foundational regional-realignment framework affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 + Personal Status Law for Non-Muslims
key_developments.description: Personal Status Law for Non-Muslims in force 1 February 2023 — establishes civil personal-status framework for all non-Muslims regardless of nationality including joint-custody default. Substantively distinctive globally first-Gulf-state-with-explicit-civil-personal-status-for-non-Muslims-by-residence-not-nationality framework.

key_developments.title: Mohammed bin Zayed presidency + UAE Cabinet 2023 framework
key_developments.description: Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan officially elected president 14 May 2022 (succeeding Khalifa bin Zayed) — substantively distinctive Gulf brotherly-succession-within-Al-Nahyan framework. Substantive UAE-Cabinet 2023 framework affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory and family-law-implementation.

key_developments.title: Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 + non-Muslim personal-status framework
key_developments.description: Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 effective 1 February 2023 establishing comprehensive non-Muslim personal-status framework distinct from Federal Law 28/2005 Muslim personal-status framework. Substantively distinctive globally Gulf bifurcated-personal-status framework — operationally significant for cross-border family-law and expatriate-community family-law adjudication. Establishes trajectory toward Federal Decree-Law 8/2024 modernisation.

key_developments.title: Federal Decree-Law 8/2024 + Federal Supreme Court substantive register
key_developments.description: Substantial amendments to Federal Law 28/2005 modernising Muslim personal-status provisions including custody, divorce, and inheritance. Federal Supreme Court + Abu Dhabi/Dubai Court of Cassation continue to develop welfare-of-the-child jurisprudence under Federal Law 28/2005 as amended + Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 + Hague Convention 1980 framework. Substantive analysis without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label adoption.

structural_findings: UAE operates a structurally distinctive dual personal-status framework — Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 for non-Muslims (civil) + Federal Law 28/2005 as amended for Muslims (religious-law). The non-Muslim framework operates by residence not nationality, substantively novel within the Gulf cluster.
Federal-emirate court architecture with DIFC + ADGM common-law commercial jurisdictions makes UAE a structurally distinctive mixed-jurisdiction model within the corpus.
Hague Convention 1980 accession 2018 places UAE in the Hague cluster — distinguishes from Saudi Arabia + Iran (non-Hague) within the Gulf cluster.

Three-tier psychology profession regulation (MOHAP federal + DOH Abu Dhabi + DHA Dubai) is structurally distinctive — multi-regulator pattern shared with Australia (federal + state) and US (state-by-state) within the corpus.

sources.title: Federal Supreme Court
sources.publisher: Federal Supreme Court of the UAE

editorial_notes: UAE jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-09 from 4 to 10 key_developments with full federation-to-MbZ trajectory: 1971-UAE-federation-+-Sheikh-Zayed + 1985-DIFC-common-law-enclave + 1997-UNCRC-ratification + 2005-Federal-Law-28-2005 + 2010-Wadeema's-Law-+-child-protection + 2018-Hague-Convention-1980-accession + 2020-Abraham-Accords-+-Israel-UAE-normalisation + 2022-Federal-Decree-Law-41-2022-+-Personal-Status-for-Non-Muslims + 2023-Mohammed-bin-Zayed-presidency + 2024-Federal-Decree-Law-8-2024-+-Federal-Supreme-Court.

Dual personal-status framework (non-Muslim civil + Muslim religious-law) + Federal Supreme Court + emirate-level apex + DIFC/ADGM common-law commercial + MOHAP/DOH/DHA three-tier psychology regulation + Hague Convention 1980 accession 2018.

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive welfare-of-the-child analysis under Federal Law 28/2005 (as amended) + Federal Decree-Law 41/2022 + Hague Convention 1980 framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins Gulf + dual-personal-status-framework-non-Muslim-civil-+-Muslim-religious-law + Federal-Decree-Law-41-2022-first-Gulf-state-with-civil-personal-status-for-non-Muslims-by-residence-not-nationality-globally-distinctive + Federal-emirate-court-architecture + DIFC-ADGM-common-law-commercial-enclaves + MOHAP-DOH-DHA-three-tier-psychology-regulation-multi-regulator (with Australia, US) + Hague-Convention-1980-accession-2018-Gulf-distinctive + Abraham-Accords-2020 + Sheikh-Zayed-founding-presidency-1971-2004 + Mohammed-bin-Zayed-2022-brotherly-succession + Wadeema's-Law-3-2016 + Federal-Decree-Law-8-2024-amendments clusters within the corpus.
